coredata.dc:title: Effect of ultra-trace Ag doping on the antibacterial performance of carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, J.
Zhu, Y.X.
Xie, X.F.
He, X.
Fan, J.T.
Chen, A.Y.
coredata.dc:description: Ag,N co‐doping carbon quantum dots (Ag,N‐CQDs) were synthesized by applying a facile hydrothermal method with green and cheap precursors of ultra‐trace silver nitrate, citric acid, and ammonia solution. The as-prepared Ag,N‐CQDs deliver effective antimicrobial performance against Gram‐negative bacteria of Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Gram‐positive bacteria of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). The microstructure of the Ag,N‐CQDs was analyzed by applying TEM, AFM, Raman spectra, and XPS spectra. Observations show that the Ag,N‐CQDs have a good crystallization structure with sizes of 5–9 nm and are assembled into nanoclusters with sizes of 10–80 nm. Antibacterial activities were determined on the basis of the colonies of E. coli and S. aureus, and the morphologies of the bacteria were observed by scanning electron microscopy. Spread plate tests show that the inhibition effect is sequenced as follows: Ag,N‐CQDs > Ag-CQDs > N‐CQDs > Ag solution. Thus, the doping of Ag in N-CQDs is a more effective way to enhance the antibacterial performance than the N-CQD and Ag solution. Furthermore, Ag,N‐CQDs display significant antibacterial activities against both bacteria colonies, while the N‐CQDs counterpart presents inhibitory effect against E. coli only. The lowest prevention concentrations of Ag,N‐CQDs are 250 and 200 μg mL−1 toward E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. The enhanced antibacterial performances of the Ag,N‐CQDs nanoclusters, especially to the S. aureus, are discussed and can be attributed to the strong adsorption of the polar chemical groups and the perforation effect of Ag atoms to disrupt bacterial membranes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ag doping
Carbon quantum dot
Nanostructure
Antibacterial performance
Adsorption

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by NSFC ( 51771121 ) and Science and Technology Committee of Shanghai Municipality ( 20ZR1437500 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the preparation of Ag,N‐CQDs.
UV‐Vis (a), PL (b), Raman (c) and FT‐IR (d) spectra of Ag,N‐CQDs and N‐CQDs. PL spectra of Ag,N‐CQDs with the dependence of excitation wavelength are inserted in (b).
TEM images of Ag,N‐CQDs. (b) and (c) are the enlarged images of the selected zones in (a) and (b), respectively. Inset in (b) is the corresponding SAED pattern.
Deconvoluted XPS spectra of the Ag,N‐CQDs. (a) C; (b) O; (c) N; (d) Ag.
The antibacterial activity of Ag,N‐CQDs to suspension bacteria in the air. (a) control; (b) Ag‐CQDs; (c) N‐CQDs; (d,e) Ag,N‐CQDs.
Agar well diffusion tests of Ag,N‐CQDs against E. coli and S. aureus. (a,c) N‐CQDs; (b,d) Ag,N‐CQDs.
O.D. of Ag,N‐CQDs against the growing E. coli and S. aureus.

CLSM images of E. coli (a–f) and S. aureus (g–l) treated by Ag,N‐CQDs. After SYTO‐9 and PI staining, the active and dead cells appear green and red, respectively. The concentration of Ag,N‐CQDs (d–f) against to E. coli is 200 μg mL−1 and that to S. aureus (j‐l) is 167 μg mL−1. All images have the same scale bar.
SEM observations of E. coli and S. aureus. (a,c) untreated control; (b,d) treated by Ag,N‐CQDs. All images have the same scale bar.
Schematic diagram of the antimicrobial effect of Ag,N‐CQDs nanoclusters.

head.ce:title: Effect of ultra-trace Ag doping on the antibacterial performance of carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science & Engineering, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai 200093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science & Engineering, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science & Engineering, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200093, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai 200050, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai, 200050, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Explosion Science and Technology, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing 100081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Explosion Science and Technology, Beijing Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Explosion Science and Technology, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing 100081, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ag,N co‐doping carbon quantum dots (Ag,N‐CQDs) were synthesized by applying a facile hydrothermal method with green and cheap precursors of ultra‐trace silver nitrate, citric acid, and ammonia solution. The as-prepared Ag,N‐CQDs deliver effective antimicrobial performance against Gram‐negative bacteria of Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Gram‐positive bacteria of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). The microstructure of the Ag,N‐CQDs was analyzed by applying TEM, AFM, Raman spectra, and XPS spectra. Observations show that the Ag,N‐CQDs have a good crystallization structure with sizes of 5–9 nm and are assembled into nanoclusters with sizes of 10–80 nm. Antibacterial activities were determined on the basis of the colonies of E. coli and S. aureus, and the morphologies of the bacteria were observed by scanning electron microscopy. Spread plate tests show that the inhibition effect is sequenced as follows: Ag,N‐CQDs > Ag-CQDs > N‐CQDs > Ag solution. Thus, the doping of Ag in N-CQDs is a more effective way to enhance the antibacterial performance than the N-CQD and Ag solution. Furthermore, Ag,N‐CQDs display significant antibacterial activities against both bacteria colonies, while the N‐CQDs counterpart presents inhibitory effect against E. coli only. The lowest prevention concentrations of Ag,N‐CQDs are 250 and 200 μg mL−1 toward E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. The enhanced antibacterial performances of the Ag,N‐CQDs nanoclusters, especially to the S. aureus, are discussed and can be attributed to the strong adsorption of the polar chemical groups and the perforation effect of Ag atoms to disrupt bacterial membranes.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, infections caused by bacteria and disease‐causing microorganisms, as well as infectious diseases caused by viruses, have become a serious global menace to public health [1–3]. Thus, the development of highly effective antibacterial materials is a serious and urgent need. Furthermore, drug resistance of bacteria and microorganisms toward existing bacteriostatic agents is increasing [4,5], especially for the main culprits of intestinal diseases and inflammatory infections of Gram‐negative bacteria of Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Gram‐positive bacteria of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) [6]. How to completely remove super bacteria and solve the increasingly bacterial drug resistance have been the mainspring in practical applications [7]. Some emerging nanomaterials, such as ZnO [7–10], CuO [11,12] and their derivatives [13,14] and silver nanoparticles and their derivatives [15–18], have been developed as effective antibacterial agents [19,20]. Silver ions can kill more than 650 species of bacteria. More importantly, they increase the sterilization effect when combined with other initiators [21]. Ghazal et al. [22] prepared silver nanoparticles with semi‐spherical shapes, exhibiting obvious inhibitory activities against E. coli and S. aureus after sputtering on cotton fabric. Although silver exhibits special antimicrobial performance [23,24], it is not fully harmless as an antimicrobial agent and is more expensive than other synthetic fungicides.

As newly developed carbon‐based antibacterial agents, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) or graphene quantum dots (GQDs), have attracted widespread attention because of their special antimicrobial performance [25–30], resulting from the good biocompatibility and nearly harmless to organisms [31]. More importantly, GQDs or CQDs are easily doped and modified by modulating different doping elements to obtain target functional groups [32]. Many interesting works were conducted to investigate the potential applications of CQDs as antimicrobial agents [33–39]. More recently, Jian et al. [37] found that CQDs synthesized from dopamine combined with spermine delivered strong inhibitory behavior toward E. coli and S. aureus, which can disrupt the bacterial membrane and possess wide-spectrum antimicrobial activity. Zhao et al. [7] reported that N‐CQDs could damage S. aureus but not E. coli. They explained that N‐CQDs could easily adsorb the bacteria with negative charges in its particular place then destroy the cell membranes. Demirci et al. [40] found that the CQDs modified by three different functional groups (−NH2, −COOH, −OH) significantly inhibited the activities of Gram‐negative and Gram‐positive bacteria. These results prove that CQDs with free radicals on the surface can transform into attractive, highly effective, and low-cytotoxicity antimicrobial agents, which make them more promising in biological imaging and other biomedical applications [39–42]. However, the doping effect of other metal atoms in the CQDs is lack of relevant works, especially to the antibacterial properties against Gram‐positive and Gram‐negative bacteria.

In this work, Ag and N co‐doped CQDs (Ag,N‐CQDs) are synthesized in the form of nanoclusters by utilizing the prominent antimicrobial performance of silver and CQDs. In particular, ultra‐trace Ag atoms at the ppm level are doped in Ag,N‐CQDs to reduce the cytotoxicity of Ag. Notably, the ultra-trace Ag doping in the CQDs brings significant progress against the two typical bacteria, especially against S. aureus, which is totally different from the N-CQDs counterpart. The antibacterial tests of the Ag,N‐CQDs demonstrate a perforation effect by Ag doping to disrupt the bacterial membrane of S. aureus, while only strong chemistry adsorption serves a function against E. coli. The effect of ultra-trace addition of Ag on antibacterial performance of CQD is discussed.

Citric acid (CA, ACS≥99.5%), ascorbic acid (C6H8O6, 99.99%), silver nitrate (AgNO3, AR), ammonia solution (NH3∙H2O, 25–28%) in analytical reagent (AR), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, AR, 30%) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. The test bacteria E. coli (ATCC25922) and S. aureus (ATCC29213) were purchased from Beijing Baierdi Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Tryptophan and yeast powders were acquired from OXOID Biologicals Co., Ltd, UK. Tryptic soy agar (TSA) was bought from Hunan Biekman Biological Technology Co. Ltd. As a first step, 15.0 g of tryptone, 5.0 g of soybean papain hydrolysate, 5.0 g of sodium chloride, and 15.0 g of agar were dissolved in 1000 mL of distilled water to obtain the TSA medium, which was kept at a pH of 7.3 ± 0.2 (25 ℃). All reagents were used as received, and all the solutions were prepared with deionized water.

The preparation of Ag,N‐CQDs is illustrated in Fig. 1. To optimize the processing parameters, the effects of Ag concentration, hydrogen peroxide, ammonia solution, reaction temperature, and time were considered. The optimized processes are provided as follows. First, 17 mg of silver nitrate and 176 mg of ascorbic acid were dissolved in 20 mL deionized water to obtain Solution A after ultrasonic stirring for 30 min. Next, 0.32 g of citric acid, 5 mL of hydrogen peroxide, and 3 mL of ammonia solution were mixed into 12 mL of deionized water under ultrasonic stirring for 30 min to prepare Solution B. Then, Solutions A (20 μL) and B (20 mL) were mixed to obtain an AgNO3 concentration of 4.25 μg mL−1. According to the report of S. Kadian et al., the osteoblasts still showed good cell activity at 112.2 mg L−1 during the evaluation of Ag@S‐GQDs [41]. Thus, the ultra‐trace Ag‐doping (4.25 μg L−1) in N-CQDs will not cause substantial damage to cell health. The blended solution was transferred into a 40 mL stainless autoclave and heated at 180 ℃ for 8 h. After filtering, Ag,N‐CQDs were collected. N‐CQDs without silver doping and Ag‐CQDs without ammonia solution were prepared under the same conditions as counterparts.

UV‐Vis curves of Ag,N‐CQDs were recorded by Lambda 750 at 200–800 nm. The photoluminescence (PL) and Raman spectra were obtained by Shimadzu RF−5301 fluorescence spectrophotometer and Horiba Jobin Yvon HR800 Raman spectrometer, respectively. The chemical groups of the as‐prepared CQDs were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared (FT‐IR) spectra at 4000–500 cm−1 through SPE Ctrum 100 FT-IR spectrometer with a scan number of 4 and a resolution of 1 cm−1. KBr pellet was used as substrate to measure the FT‐IR spectra. The microstructure of Ag,N‐CQDs was observed via high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HMTEM, TECNAI G2 F30). The Ag,N‐CQDs were imaged by atomic force microscopy (AFM, Hitachi, SPA‐400) on a silicon wafer carrier. X‐ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Nexsa) was used to determine elemental compositions.

Spread plate tests were performed, where the Ag,N‐CQDs or N‐CQDs and Ag‐CQDs solution (5 mg mL−1) were sprayed on the TSA medium by a hydraulic spray gun, then the growths of bacterial colonies were observed at room temperature. The diluted solutions of Ag,N‐CQDs and N‐CQDs were denoted as Ag,N‐CQDs‐X and N‐CQDs‐X, where X represents the dilution ratio.

The agar well diffusion tests are specified as follows. The Luria‐Bertani (LB) solid medium was heated, melted, and cooled to about 50 ℃. Then, bacterial liquid (106 CFU mL−1) was sprayed on the LB surface. After thorough mixing, the mixture was poured into a disposable sterile platter with 15 mL per platter. When the medium was cooled and solidified, holes were drilled with a sterilized hole puncher with a diameter of 9 mm, and then samples were added to the wells according to groups (150 μL per well). The diameter of the inhibition zone was surveyed with a vernier caliper after incubating at 37 ℃.

Antibacterial activities were determined by the colonies of E. coli and S. aureus. A diluted solution of Ag,N‐CQDs was added on the surface of LB with one of the above bacteria colonies. A blank control was also carried out. Optional density (O.D.) values of the Ag,N‐CQDs against the two typical bacteria were tested by a multifunctional enzyme plate analyzer at 460 nm (OD460). When the obtained O.D. value was 0.2–0.8, the bacteria were collected by centrifuge and their surface morphologies were observed by SEM. The O.D. value of each concentration of Ag,N‐CQDs was tested on 10 sets, and the average value was calculated. The distributions of active and death bacteria were imaged by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, Axio LSM700, ZEISS) after straining by SYTO‐9 green fluorescent (SYTO‐9) and propidium iodide (PI). The morphologies of the bacteria were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU 8010).

The Ag,N‐CQDs and N‐CQDs present an intensive absorption peak at 275 nm in the UV‐Vis curves ( Fig. 2a), which arises from π–π* transition of the sp2 aromatic domain (CC) [43]. The other two weak peaks at 310 and 360 nm are derived from n–π * transitions of CO and C‐N/CN bonds, respectively, which indicate the formation of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups [23,41,43]. The Ag,N‐CQDs and N‐CQDs samples both emit blue‐purple luminescence under illumination by a UV lamp (the insets in Fig. 2a), where the Ag,N‐CQDs deliver a stronger fluorescence than the N‐CQDs counterpart. The PL spectra of the Ag,N‐CQDs at different excited wavelengths are shown in the inset of Fig. 2b, where the maximum exciting wavelength is at 330 nm. The Ag,N‐CQDs also show stronger PL intensity than the N‐CQDs at 390 nm upon 330 nm excitation (Fig. 2b), which is consistent with the observation in the inset in Fig. 2a. In addition, the PL peak of Ag,N‐CQDs shows a red shift at the exciting wavelengths of 290–370 nm, as indicated in the inset of Fig. 2b, which is induced by the inhomogeneous particle sizes [7,44] and surface defect states from the functional chemical groups [33,45–47]. The inhomogeneous particle sizes and abundant functional chemical groups are confirmed by the following TEM observations and XPS analysis. The quantum yield (QY) is 9.1% of the Ag,N‐CQDs and 8.6% of the N‐CQDs according to the Eq. 1, where I, A, and n represent the emission peak area, absorption intensity, and refraction of CQDs water solution, respectively. Subscript “s” refers to the determined samples, and “r” refers to reference criteria, where the QY of quinine sulfate solution in H2SO4 (0.1 M) is 54% [43,47].
(1)
QY
s
=
QY
r
×
I
s
I
r
×
A
r
A
s

n

2
n

2

The Raman spectra (Fig. 2c) of Ag,N‐CQDs and N‐CQDs show two peaks at 1450 cm−1 (D band) and 1565 cm−1 (G band), where the D band indicates the defects of the lattice and G band indicates the in‐plane stretching vibration. The FT‐IR spectra (Fig. 2d) show that the two types of CQDs contain a wide and strong band at 3295 cm−1 from −OH groups. The intense bands at 1610, 1385, and 1040 cm−1 indicate the existence of carboxyl group (−COOH), which is attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of OC−O group. The peak at 820 cm−1 is from the stretching vibration of the C−N group, which is slightly higher than the reported peak of 807 cm−1 [48] and 809 cm−1 [49], and the red shift might be caused by the n–π conjugation of N with benzene ring [46]. These oxygen-containing groups provide the excellent dispersion ability of the Ag,N‐CQDs in solution [31,50].

Numerous nanoclusters with hollow cores are observed in the TEM image, as shown in Fig. 3a. The typical morphology of a nanocluster is given in Fig. 3b, where the walls of the nanocluster are assembled by CQDs. The selected‐area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (inset in Fig. 3b) comprises three characteristic diffraction rings originating from the (111), (122), and (311) planes, clearly indicating the nanocrystalline structure of the as‐prepared Ag,N‐CQDs. The distribution of the nanoclusters is in 10–80 nm (Supplementary Fig. S1), according to 102 particles from 12 pieces of TEM images. The distribution of particle sizes is fitted by Gauss function, and the mean particle size is 47 nm with a R-Square (coefficient of determination, COD) value of 0.973, as shown in the inset in Supplementary Fig. S1. Furthermore, the high‐resolution TEM (HRTEM) shows that the nanocluster is composed of well-crystallized CQDs with sizes of 5–9 nm (Fig. 3c). The graphene‐like structures of distinct hexagonal honeycombs are marked in the HRTEM image with the (100) facet of graphite (0.22 nm) [50]. Lattice spacings of 0.27 and 0.28 nm are also observed aside from that of 0.22 nm, which might be induced by the Ag doping. The AFM image (see Supplementary Fig. S2a) indicates that Ag,N‐CQDs present a spherical shape with sizes of 20–40 nm and a height profile of 0.6 nm (Supplementary Fig. S2b), which is close to the reported value of single‐layered graphene [51].

The XPS survey spectrum in Supplementary Fig. S3 shows the peaks of C, O, N, and Ag elements. Fig. 4a displays the deconvoluted spectrum of C 1 s, revealing the chemical states of CC at 284.8 eV, C−N at 285.8 eV, and CO/C−O at 288.7 eV [52,53]. The collection of O 1s is separated into three bands at 531.9 (CO), 533.0 (HO−CO), and 535.2 (CO/C−O) eV (Fig. 4b). These results agree with the observations of FT‐IR, confirming the formation of carboxyl and carbonyl groups on the nanoclusters. The fitted curves of N 1 s (Fig. 4c) can be identified to C−N, N − H, and CN bonds at 399.6, 400.8, and 402.2 eV [53], respectively, which might originate from imide, imine, pyridine, pyrrole, etc. [7,39]. For the Ag 3d spectrum in Fig. 4d, the noise is obvious due to the ultra-trace Ag content, while the peak of Ag 3d can still be resolved, which contains typical doublet peaks at 367.5, 368.7 eV from Ag2O 3d5/2, and 368.1, 369.8 eV from Ag 3d3/2, depicting the transition of AgNO3 into Ag and Ag2O. The generation of Ag2O might be due to the oxidation of Ag after the reaction or during the hydrothermal reaction. In comparison with the binding energy of metallic silver at 368.1 eV, the peaks are shifted to a higher level [54–56]. The work function of silver, depending on the particle size of the silver nanomaterial, is smaller than that of graphene [55,56]. Thus, electrons are transferred from metallic silver to GQDs [57–59], resulting in a shift toward higher binding energy [58,59]. The XPS results indicate that the Ag,N‐CQDs are composed of hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxylic, and N-containing groups, as well as Ag and Ag2O.

The antibacterial performance of Ag,N‐CQDs on airborne bacteria was observed on the TSA medium, which can detect the suspended bacteria in the air, as presented in Fig. 5. After standing in air for 48 h, a significant decrease of colonies on the sprayed Ag,N‐CQDs media (Fig. 5d) is observed compared with the blank control (Fig. 5a), Ag‐CQDs (Fig. 5b), and N‐CQDs (Fig. 5c). The inhibition effect of Ag,N‐CQDs on suspension bacteria in the air is still obvious after diluting from 5 mg mL−1 to 0.5 mg mL−1, as shown in Fig. 5e. Another related samples of N‐CQDs prepared by CA and urea as precursors [47] and Ag solution alone present a weak inhibition effect, as given in Supplementary Fig. S4 (b) and (e). Through a comparison of the above antibacterial effect of CQDs samples with the pure Ag solution by suspension bacteria in the air, the inhibition effect is sequenced as follows: Ag,N‐CQDs > Ag-CQDs > N‐CQDs > Ag solution. Thus, the doping of Ag in N-CQDs is the most effective way to enhance the antibacterial performance, while the Ag solution alone contributes a weaker inhibition.

The agar well diffusion tests of Ag,N‐CQDs to E. coli and S. aureus are given in Fig. 6. Ag,N‐CQDs and N‐CQDs are both effective against E. coli, while only Ag,N‐CQDs can inhibit the S. aureus. The N‐CQDs only target E. coli with an inhibition diameter of 5 mm (Fig. 6a) and exhibit no inhibition toward S. aureus (Fig. 6c). However, obvious inhibition zones appear around the Ag,N‐CQDs for both types of bacteria, where the diameters of the inhibition zones are 3 and 3 mm against E. coli (Fig. 6b) and S. aureus (Fig. 6d), respectively. Thus, Ag,N‐CQDs have an obvious antimicrobial activity against S. aureus after the doping of ultra‐trace Ag atoms. After the Ag,N‐CQDs and N‐CQDs solutions are diluted 10 times and denoted as Ag,N‐CQDs‐10 and N‐CQDs‐10, no significant inhibition zones toward the two types of bacteria are observed, as demonstrated in Fig. 6a–d. The detailed concentration‐dependent relationships of the two types of bacteria are provided in Fig. 7, where the O.D. curves of Ag,N‐CQDs deliver obvious inflection points at 250 μg mL−1 against E. coli and 200 μg mL−1 against S. aureus. These are the minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs), which are smaller than that of the recently reported works of CQDs or GQDs [41,42,59–61], as shown in Supplementary Table S1.

The CLSM images of the Ag,N‐CQDs against the two types of bacteria are presented in Fig. 8, where the SYTO‐9 makes healthy bacteria green and PI makes dead bacteria red. For the control, the green zones represent the number of the pre-set bacteria, which remains almost constant during cultivation with few dead bacteria (red). When Ag,N‐CQDs are added to the control (with the same number of green zones), the active bacteria (green zones) are inhibited, exhibiting a significant decrease of green zones. Fewer green zones mean better antibacterial performance, and fewer red zones indicate fewer dead bacteria. Compared with the control sample (Fig. 8a–c), the high activity of E. coli in the Ag,N‐CQDs sample (Fig. 8d–f) is significantly reduced accompanied with a few dead bacteria after 200 μg mL−1 of Ag,N‐CQDs were added to E. coli bacterial liquid. The same results are also found in Ag,N‐CQDs (Fig. 8j–l) against S. aureus relative to the control (Fig. 8g–i). Hence, the O.D. and CLSM images both indicate the excellent antibacterial behavior of Ag,N‐CQDs against S. aureus and E. coli, especially S. aureus.

Fig. 9 gives the SEM appearances of E. coli and S. aureus treated by Ag,N‐CQDs. Although the concentrations of Ag,N‐CQDs used for biological SEM observations were very low at 200 and 167 μg mL−1 for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively, a few Ag,N‐CQDs clusters were found on the surfaces of the two types of the bacteria, as indicated by solid arrows in Fig. 9b and d. The surfaces of untreated E. coli (Fig. 9a) are intact and smooth, whereas those treated by Ag,N‐CQDs exhibit obvious shriveling shapes as a result of dehydration (Fig. 9b). However, Fig. 9d exhibits two typical distorted morphologies of S. aureus by Ag,N‐CQDs, characterized by wrinkled (indicated by solid arrows) and broken (indicated by dotted arrows) spheres compared with the original control sample (Fig. 9c). More interestingly, nanopores are observed on the surfaces of the broken S. aureus bacteria, as indicated by dotted arrows in Fig. 9d and the inset. SEM images show that both bacteria were damaged, unlike the untreated bacteria, and underwent significant morphological changes. The perforation effect of Ag,N‐CQDs to S. aureus more greatly leads to apoptosis than cytoplasmic dehydration.

The antibacterial effects of carbon‐based materials are complex. Yun et al. [62] found that Ag‐CNT combined with Ag+ ions produced pores on the cell surface, thus enabling the composites to penetrate the bacterial cell walls. They claimed that the damage of the bacterial cell walls might be due to the irregular silver carbon nanocomposites on the outer bacterial membranes. Krishnan et al. [63] suggested that nanoparticles have a positive charge on the surface, which facilitated the bonding with the negative charge of the cytoderm, leading to cell damage. Qumar et al. [64] found that oxygen species (ROS) produced on nanoparticles, such as −COOH and −OH, could efficiently enter bacterial membranes by disrupting osmotic homeostasis and producing acute cytotoxicity, resulting in cell death by squeezing cytoplasmic contents. Hakimeh et al. [65] also mentioned this factor. Therefore, with the combination of XPS results (Fig. 4) and SEM observations (Fig. 9), the antibacterial effect of Ag,N‐CQDs can be speculated as the two following points, as explained in Fig. 10. First, the Ag,N‐CQDs nanoclusters could be easily adsorbed on the walls of the bacteria by the containing functional groups of −OH, −COOH and C−O, thus affecting the osmotic pressure between cell walls. The wrinkled surfaces of the two bacteria are produced by the adsorption of Ag,N‐CQDs, especially for E. coli. The ultra‐thin Ag,N‐CQDs, as observed in the AFM image (Supplementary Fig. S2), facilitate the adsorption on the surfaces of bacteria [65,66]. After the disorder caused by osmotic pressure, the dehydration of cell starts, leading to the inactivation and death of the bacteria, as suggested in the works of N‐CQDs [67,68]. Second, the strong interaction of the silver ions with positive charges and the cell membranes with negative charges produced holes on the binding sites, causing the divulgation of cell contents, as observed in Fig. 9d. The perforation effect of Ag doping facilitates the strong antibacterial performance of Ag,N‐CQDs against S. aureus.

In this work, an effective antimicrobial agent of Ag,N‐CQDs is developed, which exhibits strong antimicrobial performance against both E. coli and S. aureus, while the N‐CQDs counterpart shows an inhibition effect on E. coli only. The effect of Ag doping on the microstructure and antibacterial properties of Ag,N‐CQDs are systematically investigated. The following conclusions are drawn:
(1)
Ag,N‐CQDs are synthesized by using the hydrothermal method with common and green precursors of ultra‐trace silver nitrate, citric acid, hydrogen peroxide, and ammonia solution. Ag,N‐CQDs have a particle size of 5–9 nm, which assemble into the form of nanoclusters with a mean dimension of 47 nm and contain chemical groups of −OH, −COOH, and C−O, and other groups.
(2)
The Ag,N‐CQDs exhibit distinct antibacterial performance against both E. coli and S. aureus, while N‐CQDs show antibacterial effect against E. coli only. The MIC of the Ag,N‐CQDs is 250 μg mL−1 for E. coli and 200 μg mL−1 for S. aureus, respectively.
(3)
The perforation effect of Ag,N‐CQDs against S. aureus is induced by Ag doping, which is associated with the strong adsorption of Ag,N‐CQDs to negatively charged cell membranes.

J. Wang, Y. X. Zhu, and X.: He carried out the specimen preparation and performance tests. X. F. Xie, J. T. Fan, A. Y. Chen: conceived of and designed the experiments. X. He and A. Y. Chen: contributed to the data analysis and experimental design. J. Wang, Y. X. Zhu and A. Y. Chen: wrote the manuscript with contributions from the other authors. All authors commented on the final manuscript and conclusions of this work.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by NSFC (51771121) and Science and Technology Committee of Shanghai Municipality (20ZR1437500).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2021.107112.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.
